title: Spectral decomposition of $(\star, ε)$-palindromic matrix polynomial and its applications
summary: This paper provides the spectral decomposition of $(\star,ε)$-palindromic quadratic matrix polynomial $P(λ)$ by a standard pair and a parameter matrix. When $J$ is assumed to be a block diagonal matrix, the parameter matrix $Γ$ has a special structure. And then the spectral decomposition is applied to solve the inverse eigenvalue problem and the eigenvalue embedding problem with no spill-over.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the spectral decomposition of a special class of quadratic matrix polynomials and applies it to solve inverse eigenvalue and eigenvalue embedding problems without spill-over.
AI.motivation: To develop a spectral decomposition method for ($\star,ε$)-palindromic quadratic matrix polynomials and solve related inverse eigenvalue problems with no spill-over.
AI.method: The paper uses a standard pair and a parameter matrix to derive the spectral decomposition, and assumes J is block diagonal to give the parameter matrix Γ a special structure.
AI.result: The spectral decomposition is successfully applied to solve the inverse eigenvalue problem and the eigenvalue embedding problem with no spill-over.
AI.conclusion: The spectral decomposition of ($\star,ε$)-palindromic quadratic matrix polynomials is useful for solving inverse eigenvalue problems without spill-over.

title: Deep-Picard Iteration for Space-time Fractional Diffusion PDEs
summary: We propose a Deep-Picard iteration framework for high-dimensional nonlinear space-time fractional diffusion equations.The method is based on a nonlinear fractional Feynman--Kac fixed-point formulation, which replaces direct discretization of the Caputo memory term and the nonlocal fractional Laplacian by Monte Carlo simulation of the associated fractional dynamics. Each Picard update is approximated by stochastic label generation and realized through supervised neural-network regression, thereby avoiding residual minimization involving fractional differential operators. The fractional trajectories are generated by coupling a discretized beta-stable subordinator with a walk-on-spheres-type simulation of the rotationally symmetric alpha-stable Lévy process. Numerical experiments on two-dimensional and high-dimensional test problems ddemonstrate stable Picard convergence and accurate approximation, with tests reported up to dimension d=100.

AI.tldr: A Deep-Picard iteration framework using Monte Carlo simulation and neural-network regression solves high-dimensional nonlinear space-time fractional diffusion equations, avoiding direct operator discretization, with accurate results up to dimension d=100.
AI.motivation: To efficiently solve high-dimensional nonlinear space-time fractional diffusion equations, which are challenging due to nonlocal fractional operators and the curse of dimensionality.
AI.method: The method uses a nonlinear fractional Feynman-Kac fixed-point formulation, Picard iteration with supervised neural-network regression for updates, and Monte Carlo simulation of fractional trajectories via a discretized beta-stable subordinator coupled with walk-on-spheres simulation of alpha-stable Lévy processes.
AI.result: Stable Picard convergence and accurate approximation demonstrated on 2D and high-dimensional test problems, with experiments reported up to dimension d=100.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively overcomes challenges of high-dimensional fractional PDEs by replacing direct operator discretization with Monte Carlo and neural network techniques.

title: Characterization of ELM Pacing via Vertical Jogs on DIII-D
summary: Edge localized mode (ELM) pacing via vertical plasma oscillations or jogging has been successfully demonstrated on DIII-D. Rapid vertical movement of the plasma toward the X-point has been shown to effectively trigger ELMs. By vertically oscillating the plasma at a rate of 20 Hz, the ELM frequency increased from $\sim$5~Hz, the natural ELM frequency in similar DIII-D discharges, to 20~Hz. Downward jogs have been observed to trigger multiple ELMs in one cycle. ELMs triggered at higher than natural frequencies lead to smaller decreases in stored energy, from ~10\% to as little as below 1\%. As a consequence, the peak heat flux to the divertor has been observed to be reduced by a factor of $\sim$2. In addition, a reduction in the carbon impurity concentration has been observed. During downward jogs in the lower single null (LSN) configuration, the X-point movement is slower and smaller than the top of the plasma. As a result, a reduction in the plasma cross section and hence volume has been observed. To understand the mechanism of ELM triggering by jogging, a toy model of the edge toroidal current has been built and tested with DIII-D experiment data. The experimental data and model suggest that when the plasma moves down towards the X-point, a net positive toroidal current is locally induced in the edge region. ELITE stability analysis suggests that this current pushes the plasma state across the peeling side of the peeling-ballooning stability boundary into the unstable region triggering ELMs.

AI.tldr: Vertical plasma jogging on DIII-D effectively paces ELMs by inducing edge toroidal currents that push the plasma across the peeling-ballooning stability boundary, achieving higher ELM frequency (20 Hz vs. ~5 Hz natural), reduced energy loss (~1-10%), lower divertor heat flux (~2x reduction), and decreased carbon impurity concentration.
AI.motivation: To control and mitigate large, damaging ELMs in tokamaks by developing a pacing technique that triggers smaller, more frequent ELMs, thereby reducing heat flux to the divertor and impurity concentration.
AI.method: Vertical plasma oscillations/jogging at 20 Hz on DIII-D to trigger ELMs; experimental measurement of ELM frequency, stored energy drops, heat flux, and impurity concentrations; development of a toy model of edge toroidal current; ELITE stability analysis to assess peeling-ballooning stability boundary crossing.
AI.result: ELM frequency increased from ~5 Hz to 20 Hz; stored energy drop reduced from ~10% to below 1%; divertor peak heat flux reduced by ~2x; carbon impurity concentration decreased; X-point movement slower and smaller than top of plasma; net positive toroidal current induced locally at edge during downward jogs.
AI.conclusion: Vertical jogging is an effective ELM pacing method. The triggering mechanism is explained by locally induced edge toroidal currents that push the plasma across the peeling-ballooning stability boundary, leading to triggered ELMs at higher frequencies with reduced adverse effects.

title: MuDirac 1.3.0: A Sustainable Software Tool for Calculating Ground State Nuclear Properties Using Muonic X-Ray Measurements
comment: 37 pages, 16 figures. Manuscript on use of negative muons for estimating nuclear properties
summary: The nuclear charge radius is one of the most fundamental quantities of the atomic nucleus. It can be deduced from a combination of experimental measurements of muonicX-raytransitionenergieswithmodellingofthoseX-raytransitionenergies. In thisworkwepresentMuDirac (1.3.0), whichisanopen, publiclyavailable, sustainable and computationally efficient software tool that will be at put the disposal of the negative muon community. With MuDirac (1.3.0), the community will be able to accurately and efficiently estimate nuclear properties, such as the nuclear charge radius, by assuming a 2-parameter Fermi distribution of the nuclear charge.

AI.tldr: This paper presents MuDirac (1.3.0), an open-source software tool for estimating nuclear charge radii by analyzing muonic X-ray transition energies using a 2-parameter Fermi distribution model.
AI.motivation: To provide the negative muon community with an open, publicly available, sustainable, and computationally efficient tool for accurately estimating fundamental nuclear properties like the nuclear charge radius from muonic X-ray measurements.
AI.method: The software assumes a 2-parameter Fermi distribution of the nuclear charge to model muonic X-ray transition energies and deduce nuclear charge radii from experimental measurements.
AI.result: The tool MuDirac (1.3.0) enables accurate and efficient estimation of nuclear properties from muonic X-ray data.
AI.conclusion: MuDirac provides a sustainable, open, and efficient computational solution for the negative muon community to estimate nuclear charge radii and related properties.

title: Formation and Behavior of Dirac Singularities in the Parabolic-Elliptic Keller-Segel System in Dimensions $n\geq 3$
summary: We consider nonnegative radially symmetric solutions of the parabolic-elliptic Keller-Segel system \begin{align*} \left\lbrace \begin{array}{r@{}l@{\quad}l} &u_t=Δu-\nabla \cdot \big(u\nabla v\big),\\ &0=Δv -μ+ u , \\ \end{array}\right. \end{align*} where $μ$ is the spatial average of $u$, under homogeneous Neumann boundary conditions in a ball in $\mathbb R^n$ for $n\geq 3$. In two dimensions, it is well established that solutions blowing up in finite time converge to a Dirac profile in the vague topology. In contrast, for $n\geq 3$, blow-up solutions with finite existence time do not appear to exhibit such concentration behavior. By generalizing to measure-valued solutions corresponding to accumulated densities of $u$, we extend the analysis beyond the blow-up time. Within this framework, we establish the existence of a minimal solution \[ u(t)=θ(t)δ_0 + ρ(\cdot,t) dx, \qquad t \geq 0, \] where $ρ$ is integrable and $θ$ is increasing and right-continuous. We further construct a class of initial data for which $θ(t_0)>0$ for some $t_0>0$, thereby establishing the formation of a Dirac mass at the origin. Unlike in the case $n=2$, the singular mass does not jump to a positive level instantaneously; instead, $θ$ becomes positive continuously. Moreover, $θ$ is strictly increasing on $[t_0,\infty)$, and the entire mass is asymptotically absorbed at the origin.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the Keller-Segel system in dimensions n≥3, showing that unlike in 2D, blow-up solutions do not concentrate into a Dirac mass at the blow-up time. By extending to measure-valued solutions, the authors prove existence of a minimal solution with a Dirac component that emerges continuously and grows strictly, with all mass eventually absorbed at the origin.
AI.motivation: To understand the qualitative difference in blow-up behavior of the Keller-Segel system between 2D (where Dirac concentration occurs at blow-up) and higher dimensions n≥3 (where such instantaneous concentration does not happen), and to develop a framework for analyzing solutions beyond blow-up time.
AI.method: The authors generalize to measure-valued solutions representing accumulated densities of u, allowing continuation past blow-up time. They construct a minimal solution of the form u(t)=θ(t)δ₀+ρ(·,t)dx with increasing right-continuous θ and integrable ρ.
AI.result: In n≥3, Dirac mass formation occurs continuously (not instantaneously as in 2D). The singular part θ becomes positive at some finite time t₀>0 and is strictly increasing on [t₀,∞), with all mass asymptotically concentrated at the origin.
AI.conclusion: Higher-dimensional Keller-Segel blow-up differs fundamentally from the 2D case: mass concentration is a gradual process rather than an instantaneous jump, providing new insight into singularity formation in chemotaxis models.

title: Continuous 3D Finite Element Subgrid Basis Functions for Discontinuous Galerkin Methods on Polyhedral Meshes
summary: We present a novel high-order accurate nodal discontinuous Galerkin (DG) method for solving nonlinear hyperbolic systems of partial differential equations (PDEs) on fully unstructured three-dimensional polyhedral meshes. A mesh generator is firstly discussed in detail, which ensures the generation of admissible control volumes. For the first time, we then extend the concept of agglomerated finite element (AFE) basis functions to polyhedral grids. In this context, the discrete solution is represented within each polyhedral element using piecewise continuous polynomials of degree N, defined on an internal tetrahedral subgrid. The AFE basis functions are therefore constructed by agglomerating standard finite element basis functions on each sub-tetrahedron of the computational cell. This allows for the precomputation of universal local matrices (mass and stiffness) on the reference element given by the unit tetrahedron, enabling a quadrature-free implementation that remains efficient even on highly irregular polyhedral meshes. High-order of accuracy in time is achieved using a local spacetime Galerkin predictor as part of the ADER approach, applied independently within each polyhedral element. To ensure robustness in the presence of discontinuities such as shocks, an artificial viscosity limiter is embedded into the numerical scheme, allowing for controlled dissipation and stabilization without compromising the overall accuracy in smooth regions. To demonstrate the robustness and accuracy of the method, we validate it through different three-dimensional benchmark problems for the compressible Euler and Navier-Stokes equations.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a high-order discontinuous Galerkin method for solving nonlinear hyperbolic PDEs on unstructured 3D polyhedral meshes, using agglomerated finite element basis functions, quadrature-free implementation, ADER time-stepping, and an artificial viscosity limiter, validated on Euler and Navier-Stokes benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To enable high-order accurate DG methods on fully unstructured three-dimensional polyhedral meshes, which are more flexible for complex geometries but challenging for conventional DG approaches due to irregular element shapes.
AI.method: The method uses a mesh generator for admissible polyhedral control volumes, extends agglomerated finite element (AFE) basis functions to polyhedral grids with piecewise continuous polynomials on internal tetrahedral subgrids, employs a quadrature-free implementation via precomputed reference element matrices, and applies ADER local spacetime Galerkin predictor for high-order time accuracy with an artificial viscosity limiter for shock capturing.
AI.result: Validated through various 3D benchmark problems for compressible Euler and Navier-Stokes equations, demonstrating robustness and accuracy of the method on polyhedral meshes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed DG method with AFE basis functions is effective and robust for high-order accurate simulations on highly irregular polyhedral meshes, suitable for complex 3D problems with discontinuities.

title: The L-H transition in tokamaks: power threshold, density minimum and toroidal-field asymmetry
summary: The physical mechanism underlying the L--H transition in tokamaks has remained an open problem for over forty years. We present three-dimensional flux-driven two-fluid simulations in a diverted geometry that exhibit a confinement transition at lower power in the favourable toroidal-field configuration. The simulations show that electromagnetic drift-wave turbulence spontaneously generates a sheared $\bm{E}\times \bm{B}$ flow responsible for transport suppression. The toroidal-field-direction asymmetry arises from time-reversal symmetry breaking by finite collisionality, as demonstrated by a quasilinear calculation of the turbulent momentum flux. First-principles scaling laws are derived for the L--H power threshold in both density branches, the density minimum, and the minimum power, all matching or surpassing existing empirical scalings.

AI.tldr: This paper presents 3D flux-driven two-fluid simulations that reveal the physical mechanism behind the L-H transition in tokamaks, showing that electromagnetic drift-wave turbulence spontaneously generates sheared E×B flows responsible for transport suppression, and derives first-principles scaling laws for the power threshold that match empirical observations.
AI.motivation: To solve the forty-year-old open problem of the physical mechanism underlying the L-H transition in tokamaks, particularly the toroidal-field-direction asymmetry in the power threshold.
AI.method: Three-dimensional flux-driven two-fluid simulations in diverted geometry, combined with quasilinear calculation of turbulent momentum flux to analyze time-reversal symmetry breaking by finite collisionality.
AI.result: Simulations show confinement transition at lower power in favorable toroidal-field configuration; electromagnetic drift-wave turbulence spontaneously generates sheared E×B flow; first-principles scaling laws match or surpass empirical scalings for power threshold, density branches, density minimum, and minimum power.
AI.conclusion: The toroidal-field-direction asymmetry arises from time-reversal symmetry breaking by finite collisionality, providing a first-principles explanation for the long-standing L-H transition problem.

title: Unsupervised Learning of Quantum Phase Transitions for Bose-Hubbard lattice systems
summary: Characterizing quantum many-body phase structure is a major goal for quantum simulation. Here, we employ an unsupervised learning approach based on diffusion maps to learn phase transitions in bosonic lattice systems described by Bose-Hubbard type models, which can be realized in ultracold atoms and related quantum simulation platforms. We demonstrate that this approach identifies phase structure across distinct settings without prior knowledge of order parameters or handcrafted observables, including ground-state transitions involving symmetry-protected topological phases and nonequilibrium regimes distinguishing ergodic and many-body localized behavior. Our results indicate that the approach has the potential for direct application to experimentally accessible measurement data for learning quantum phases in current quantum simulators.

AI.tldr: The paper uses diffusion maps, an unsupervised learning method, to identify phase transitions in Bose-Hubbard models, covering both ground-state (including symmetry-protected topological phases) and nonequilibrium (ergodic vs. many-body localization) regimes, without requiring prior knowledge of order parameters.
AI.motivation: Characterizing quantum many-body phase structure is a major goal for quantum simulation; existing approaches often rely on handcrafted observables or prior knowledge of order parameters, which may be unknown for complex or novel phases.
AI.method: An unsupervised learning approach based on diffusion maps is applied to bosonic lattice systems described by Bose-Hubbard type models, learning phase transitions directly from data without order parameter knowledge.
AI.result: The method successfully identifies phase structure across distinct settings, including ground-state transitions involving symmetry-protected topological phases and nonequilibrium regimes distinguishing ergodic and many-body localized behavior.
AI.conclusion: The approach has potential for direct application to experimentally accessible measurement data for learning quantum phases in current quantum simulators.

title: Local Asymptotic Patterns for Viscous Approximations of Conservation Laws
summary:
Solutions to hyperbolic conservation laws can be approximated in many different ways: by vanishing viscosity, relaxations, discrete or semi-discrete numerical schemes, approximation with a nonlocal flux, etc$\ldots$ For some of these methods, general ${\bf L}^1$ convergence results are available. Aim of this paper is to understand the local behavior of these approximations, in a neighborhood of point where the hyperbolic solution has a singularity. Specifically: a point along a shock, or where two shocks interact, or where a new shock is formed.
  Given a sequence of $ε$-approximate solutions, a general expectation is that, by a suitable local rescaling of coordinates, as $ε\to 0$ a well defined limit is obtained. This corresponds to a specific ``eternal solution" (globally defined both in space and in time) to the approximating equation. Precise results this direction are here given, in the case of vanishing viscosity.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the local behavior of approximations to hyperbolic conservation laws near singularities (shocks, shock interactions, shock formation) by analyzing how ε-approximate solutions converge, via local rescaling, to eternal solutions. Focus is on vanishing viscosity approximations.
AI.motivation: While global L¹ convergence of approximations to hyperbolic conservation laws is well understood, the local behavior near singularities (shock points, shock interactions, shock formation) remains unclear. This paper aims to characterize these local limits through rescaling.
AI.method: A sequence of ε-approximate solutions is studied. By applying suitable local rescaling of coordinates near singular points, the limit as ε→0 is examined. The focus is on vanishing viscosity approximations, showing these rescaled limits correspond to eternal solutions (defined globally in space and time).
AI.result: Under appropriate local rescaling, the ε-approximate solutions converge to well-defined eternal solutions of the approximating equation in the vanishing viscosity limit, providing a precise characterization of local behavior near singularities.
AI.conclusion: The local structure of vanishing viscosity approximations near shock singularities is given by eternal solutions obtained via rescaling, offering a rigorous understanding of these limiting profiles.

title: Reliability, Robustness, and Resilience Modeling for Surveillance System in Advanced Air Mobility Operations
summary: Ensuring the safe and efficient operation of Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) in low-altitude airspace requires a reliable, robust, and resilient surveillance system capable of continuously detecting, identifying, and tracking aircraft under both normal and off-nominal conditions. To address this need, this study develops a comprehensive 3R modeling framework, reliability, robustness, and resilience, for the Surveillance for Advanced Air Mobility (SAM) system, with a focus on the optimal design and operation of a multi-type sensor network. Under normal operating conditions, the reliability model determines the baseline sensor types, quantities, and locations required to satisfy surveillance coverage and detection requirements. To address external perturbations, such as adverse weather conditions or sudden increases in AAM traffic demand, the robustness model identifies additional sensor requirements needed to maintain system performance. Furthermore, for surveillance outages caused by primary sensor failures, the resiliency model develops backup sensor deployment and dispatch strategies to provide temporary surveillance coverage, minimize operational disruptions, and support the safe continuation of AAM operations.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a 3R (Reliability, Robustness, Resilience) modeling framework for the optimal design and operation of multi-type sensor networks in Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) surveillance systems. It addresses normal operations, external perturbations (e.g., adverse weather, traffic surges), and primary sensor failures with corresponding sensor deployment strategies.
AI.motivation: Safe and efficient AAM operations require a surveillance system that is reliable, robust, and resilient to continuously detect, identify, and track aircraft under both normal and off-nominal conditions.
AI.method: A comprehensive 3R modeling framework (Reliability, Robustness, Resilience) for SAM system, focusing on optimal design and operation of multi-type sensor networks.
AI.result: Reliability model determines baseline sensor types/quantities/locations for normal conditions; robustness model identifies additional sensors for external perturbations; resiliency model develops backup deployment/dispatch strategies for primary sensor failures.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides end-to-end sensor network design that ensures surveillance coverage across normal operations, external disturbances, and failure scenarios, supporting safe AAM operations.

title: Human-in-the-Loop Meta Bayesian Optimization for Fusion Energy and Scientific Applications
comment: Accepted at IJCAI 2026 (35th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence)
summary: Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF) holds transformative promise for sustainable, near-limitless clean energy, yet remains constrained by prohibitively high costs and limited experimental opportunities. This paper presents Human-in-the-Loop Meta Bayesian Optimization (HL-MBO), a framework that integrates expert knowledge with few-shot, uncertainty-aware machine learning to accelerate discovery in data-scarce, high-stakes scientific domains. HL-MBO introduces a meta-learned surrogate model with an expert-informed acquisition function to recommend candidate experiments. To foster trust and enable informed decisions, HL-MBO also provides interpretable explanations of its suggestions. We show HL-MBO outperforms current BO methods on ICF energy yield optimization, as well as benchmarks in molecular optimization and critical temperature maximization for superconducting materials.

AI.tldr: HL-MBO integrates expert knowledge with meta-learned Bayesian optimization to accelerate discovery in data-scarce scientific domains like Inertial Confinement Fusion, outperforming standard BO methods across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: ICF holds promise for clean energy but is constrained by high costs and limited experiments. The need to accelerate discovery in data-scarce, high-stakes scientific domains drives this work.
AI.method: HL-MBO combines a meta-learned surrogate model with an expert-informed acquisition function to recommend experiments, and provides interpretable explanations to foster trust.
AI.result: HL-MBO outperforms current BO methods on ICF energy yield optimization, molecular optimization, and critical temperature maximization for superconducting materials.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully accelerates scientific discovery in data-constrained settings by incorporating human expertise into Bayesian optimization with interpretability.

title: Structure-Preserving Optimal Control of Maxwell's Equations with Applications to Source Cloaking
summary: We develop a structure-preserving solution framework for the optimal control of the time-dependent Maxwell's equations. Building on a well-posedness theory for a weak form of the forward problem, we first analyze a forward solver that couples Nédélec and Raviart--Thomas finite elements with Crank--Nicolson time stepping. The solver preserves the de~Rham structure, enforces a discrete Gauss law, exactly satisfies a per-time-step energy balance, and converges to the weak solution under low regularity assumptions on the problem data, which are dictated by the optimal control setting. To control the Maxwell system, we add the curl of a space-time current density as a source to Ampére's law. The curl form yields charge conservation without auxiliary constraints. We prove the well-posedness and continuity of the control-to-state map, derive the adjoint system and a gradient representation for a tracking-type objective functional, and formulate a discrete optimization scheme that inherits structure preservation from the forward solver. Our discrete stationarity conditions are consistent with their continuous counterparts, and the discrete optimal controls converge, with mesh and time refinements, to the continuous optima. We demonstrate the merits of our optimal control formulation and the theoretical developments by numerically solving a series of source-cloaking model problems.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a structure-preserving finite element framework for optimal control of time-dependent Maxwell's equations, using Nédélec and Raviart-Thomas elements with Crank-Nicolson time stepping, and demonstrates it on source-cloaking problems.
AI.motivation: To design an optimal control framework for time-dependent Maxwell's equations that preserves the underlying de Rham structure, ensures physical conservation laws (charge, energy), and achieves convergence under low regularity assumptions suitable for control settings.
AI.method: Couples Nédélec and Raviart-Thomas finite elements with Crank-Nicolson time stepping to preserve de Rham structure; adds curl of space-time current density as source to Ampére's law for charge conservation; derives adjoint system and gradient representation for tracking-type objective functional.
AI.result: Proves well-posedness and continuity of control-to-state map; shows discrete stationarity conditions are consistent with continuous ones; demonstrates convergence of discrete optimal controls to continuous optima under mesh/time refinements; validates via source-cloaking numerical examples.
AI.conclusion: The structure-preserving approach successfully enforces discrete Gauss law, exact per-time-step energy balance, and charge conservation without auxiliary constraints, while maintaining convergence properties essential for optimal control of Maxwell systems.

title: Gradient blowup of smooth vacuum solutions to 1D compressible Euler equations
summary: We consider the isentropic compressible Euler equations in the half-line which govern the motion of gaseous fluids in contact with stationary vacuum boundary. We construct a large class of solutions that are initially smooth and square-integrable, and which, in finite time, transition to $C^{1-μ}$ regularity for $μ\in [1/2,1)$ near the boundary, leading to the gradient blowup at the boundary. It is based on stability analysis of self-similar waiting time solutions \cite{JLN2025} recently constructed by the authors.

AI.tldr: This paper studies smooth solutions to compressible Euler equations that develop gradient blowup at a vacuum boundary in finite time, transitioning to $C^{1-\mu}$ regularity.
AI.motivation: To understand the finite-time regularity transition and gradient blowup phenomena at vacuum boundaries in compressible fluid flows.
AI.method: Stability analysis of self-similar waiting time solutions previously constructed by the authors.
AI.result: Constructs a large class of solutions that are initially smooth and square-integrable, but transition in finite time to $C^{1-\mu}$ regularity ($\mu\in[1/2,1)$) near the boundary, with gradient blowup at the boundary.
AI.conclusion: The transition to lower regularity and gradient blowup at vacuum boundaries can occur in finite time for compressible Euler equations, and these solutions are stable in a certain sense.

title: A Homotopy Framework for Constrained Multiobjective Optimization
summary: We develop a homotopy-based framework for computing Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) points of multiobjective optimization problems. The proposed homotopy map continuously deforms an easily solvable system into the KKT conditions associated with the multiobjective problem, yielding a deterministic and structure-preserving continuation path. Under mild regularity assumptions, we establish global convergence of the homotopy trajectory to a Pareto-stationary solution for any initial point chosen in the interior of the feasible region. In numerical experiments, the method exhibits robust convergence even when initialized from nonfeasible points, indicating stability beyond the theoretical guarantees. Efficient predictor-corrector continuation strategies are employed to trace the homotopy path. Numerical results on benchmark problems compare the proposed approach with classical scalarization methods and the evolutionary algorithm NSGA-II, demonstrating competitive computational efficiency and consistent solution quality. These results highlight the effectiveness of the homotopy framework for constrained multiobjective optimization and motivate extensions to more general problem settings and adaptive parameter strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a homotopy-based framework for computing KKT points of constrained multiobjective optimization problems. The method deforms an easy system into the KKT conditions, guaranteeing global convergence to Pareto-stationary solutions under mild assumptions. Numerical experiments show robust convergence and competitive performance against scalarization methods and NSGA-II.
AI.motivation: To develop a deterministic, structure-preserving method for finding Pareto-stationary solutions in constrained multiobjective optimization, overcoming limitations of stochastic evolutionary algorithms and scalarization approaches.
AI.method: A homotopy map continuously deforms a simple solvable system into the KKT conditions of the multiobjective problem. Predictor-corrector continuation strategies trace the homotopy path, with global convergence established under mild regularity assumptions.
AI.result: Robust convergence even from nonfeasible initial points; competitive computational efficiency and solution quality compared to classical scalarization methods and NSGA-II on benchmark problems.
AI.conclusion: The homotopy framework is effective for constrained multiobjective optimization, with potential for extensions to more general settings and adaptive parameter strategies.

title: Magnetic loops in the solar transition region
comment: An invited review to appear on RMPP, 32 pages
summary: Transition region (TR) loops are arcade-like features in the solar transition region, with temperatures roughly between $2\times10^4$ K and $6\times10^5$ K. They are a fundamental building block of TR, which are results of the coupling between the magnetic field and the TR plasma. Their dynamics is closely related to the transport of energy and mass through the TR. Studies on this class of loops since the launch of the Interface Region Imaging Spectrograph (IRIS) have revealed that they are distinct from coronal loops. Observations have revealed that they are associated with many small-scale dynamic phenomena in the TR, from which one can infer the physics behind the energy and mass transfer in a magnetically confined environment at TR temperature. This review summarises the observational results of TR loops, showing their morphology, dynamics, plasma parameters, their relationship with flux emergence, their heating properties, and their implication in the heating of the solar atmosphere. This class of magnetic loops is much less well understood than their coronal counterparts. This review also concludes with several critical questions that need to be answered in the coming era with more advanced observational techniques and more precise and realistic simulations.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews transition region loops—arcade-like structures at 2×10⁴–6×10⁵ K—observed by IRIS, summarizing their morphology, dynamics, plasma parameters, flux emergence relationships, heating properties, and role in solar atmosphere heating, while highlighting open questions for future observations and simulations.
AI.motivation: TR loops are fundamental building blocks of the solar transition region, yet they are much less understood than coronal loops. Understanding their dynamics is crucial for uncovering energy and mass transport mechanisms through the transition region and their role in solar atmosphere heating.
AI.method: This is a review paper that synthesizes observational results from the Interface Region Imaging Spectrograph (IRIS) and other instruments, covering morphology, dynamics, plasma diagnostics, and statistical relationships with flux emergence.
AI.result: TR loops are distinct from coronal loops, exhibit small-scale dynamic phenomena, and are closely tied to energy/mass transfer in magnetically confined TR plasma. Their heating properties have implications for solar atmosphere heating.
AI.conclusion: Several critical questions remain about TR loops, requiring more advanced observational techniques and more precise, realistic simulations to be answered in the future.

title: Scale-Aware Adversarial Analysis: A Diagnostic for Generative AI in Multiscale Complex Systems
summary: Complex physical systems, from supersonic turbulence to the macroscopic structure of the universe, are governed by continuous multiscale dynamics. While modern machine learning architectures excel at mapping the high-dimensional observables of these systems, it remains unclear whether they internalize the governing physical laws or merely interpolate discrete statistical correlations. Standard Explainable AI (XAI) architectures, particularly perturbation-based and gradient-saliency methods, rely on pixel-wise perturbations, which generate unphysical artifacts and push inputs off the valid empirical distribution. To resolve this, we introduce a diagnostic framework driven by Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD), a diffusion-based multiscale data decomposition algorithm that enables physically constrained data generation and model evaluation via scale-aware modifications. Applying this framework to a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM), we execute deterministic interventions directly within the continuous, CDD-based scale space. We demonstrate that under moderate physical perturbations, the unconstrained generative model exhibits localized structural freezing and non-linear instability rather than continuous PDE-like responses. The network fails to maintain cross-scale continuity, causing the generative trajectory to diverge when pushed into unseen physical states. By synthesizing a continuum of physically coherent states, this scale-informed methodology establishes a controlled test ground to evaluate algorithmic vulnerabilities, providing the rigorous physical constraints necessary for future architectures to respect the multiscale causality of the natural universe.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD), a diffusion-based multiscale framework for diagnosing whether ML models internalize physical laws. Applied to DDPMs, it reveals that unconstrained models fail under physically coherent perturbations, exhibiting structural freezing and instability instead of continuous PDE-like behavior.
AI.motivation: Standard XAI methods rely on pixel-wise perturbations that generate unphysical artifacts and push inputs off the valid empirical distribution, making them unsuitable for diagnosing whether models internalize governing physical laws of complex multiscale systems.
AI.method: A diagnostic framework using Constrained Diffusion Decomposition (CDD), a diffusion-based multiscale decomposition algorithm that enables physically constrained data generation and scale-aware model evaluation. Applied to a DDPM via deterministic interventions in the continuous CDD scale space.
AI.result: Under moderate physical perturbations, the unconstrained generative model shows localized structural freezing and non-linear instability rather than continuous PDE-like responses. The network fails to maintain cross-scale continuity, causing trajectory divergence in unseen physical states.
AI.conclusion: The scale-informed CDD methodology establishes a controlled testbed for evaluating algorithmic vulnerabilities, providing the physical constraints needed for future architectures to respect multiscale causality in natural systems.

title: Existence and uniqueness of weak solutions to quasilinear PDEs with critical data
summary: We establish existence and uniqueness of global, bounded weak solutions to quasilinear PDEs with bounded, uniformly continuous initial data and investigate their properties. Moreover, we establish existence of bounded weak solutions when the initial data is merely bounded.

AI.tldr: The paper proves existence and uniqueness of global bounded weak solutions for quasilinear PDEs under different initial data conditions, with varying smoothness requirements.
AI.motivation: To establish rigorous theoretical foundations for quasilinear PDEs with bounded initial data, particularly when the initial data is only bounded and not necessarily continuous.
AI.method: Analytical techniques for quasilinear PDEs, establishing existence and uniqueness through weak solution frameworks with varying regularity assumptions on initial data.
AI.result: (1) Existence and uniqueness of global bounded weak solutions for bounded uniformly continuous initial data; (2) Existence (but not necessarily uniqueness) of bounded weak solutions when initial data is merely bounded.
AI.conclusion: The paper advances the theory of quasilinear PDEs by relaxing regularity requirements on initial data while still ensuring existence of bounded weak solutions.

title: Reconstruction of glymphatic transport fields from subject-specific imaging data, with particular emphasis on cerebrospinal fluid flow and tracer conservation
comment: Total 40 pages including references and appendix, 16 figures
summary: The reconstruction of physically valid transport fields from subject-specific imaging data is a fundamental challenge in image-based computational modeling due to measurement noise, modeling uncertainties and discretization errors. Without a methodology to construct models that faithfully reflect the underlying physics, mechanistic understanding of complex biological systems is inherently limited. In this work, we address this challenge in the glymphatic system, the brain's waste-clearance network, where cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is transported through perivascular spaces into the brain parenchyma to facilitate metabolic waste removal. We introduce a computational framework for the high-fidelity reconstruction of subject-specific glymphatic transport fields from spatiotemporal imaging data. The formulation utilizes an advection-diffusion model with a velocity decomposition that imposes mass conservation, enabling the recovery of solenoidal (divergence-free) velocity fields through the solution of a constrained inverse problem. The system is discretized using immersed isogeometric analysis with quadratic B-spline basis functions, providing smooth, high-continuity solutions and inherent regularization of imaging noise. We demonstrate the framework's utility by using contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging of tracer transport in a mouse brain, obtaining spatially varying estimates of CSF velocity, diffusivity, and clearance parameters. Forward simulations using the recovered fields show close agreement with experimental observations, validating the framework's ability to characterize complex transport dynamics while preserving physical integrity. This approach provides a generalizable methodology for the robust inference of physically consistent transport fields from imperfect imaging data, with broad applicability to the image-guided modeling of biological and engineering systems.

AI.tldr: A computational framework reconstructs physically valid CSF transport fields from spatiotemporal imaging data using an advection-diffusion model with velocity decomposition and immersed isogeometric analysis, demonstrated on contrast-enhanced MRI of a mouse brain.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing physically valid transport fields from noisy, uncertain imaging data is a fundamental challenge limiting mechanistic understanding of biological systems, particularly in the glymphatic system (brain's waste-clearance network).
AI.method: An advection-diffusion model with velocity decomposition imposing mass conservation, solved as a constrained inverse problem; discretized using immersed isogeometric analysis with quadratic B-spline basis functions for smooth, high-continuity solutions and noise regularization.
AI.result: Spatially varying estimates of CSF velocity, diffusivity, and clearance parameters were obtained; forward simulations showed close agreement with experimental contrast-enhanced MRI observations in a mouse brain.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a generalizable methodology for robust, physics-consistent inference of transport fields from imperfect imaging data, applicable to image-guided modeling of biological and engineering systems.

title: Almost global large deviations principle for the KdV equation
summary: We study extreme wave formation for the Korteweg-de Vries equation on the torus with random initial data of average size $ε$. We establish a large deviations principle for the supremum of the solution over arbitrarily long polynomial timescales $t \leq ε^{-n}$ for any fixed natural number $n$. This identifies the leading-order asymptotics of the probability of observing unusually large amplitudes. In this integrable setting, the dynamics evolves on invariant tori where Fourier moduli are almost conserved, ruling out mechanisms for extreme wave formation based on resonant energy exchange. As a result, large amplitudes can only arise through coherent structures or dispersive focusing, which corresponds to the quasi-synchronization of many phases. We show that the latter is dominant in the weakly nonlinear regime. Our approach combines a Birkhoff normal form analysis with probabilistic arguments, exploiting the stability of the integrable dynamics to control the probability of phase quasi-synchronization over long timescales.

AI.tldr: This paper studies extreme wave formation in the Korteweg-de Vries equation on a torus with random initial data of small average size ε. It establishes a large deviations principle for the supremum of the solution over polynomial timescales t ≤ ε^{-n}, showing that large amplitudes arise primarily through dispersive focusing (phase quasi-synchronization) rather than resonant energy exchange. The approach combines Birkhoff normal form analysis with probabilistic arguments.
AI.motivation: To understand how extreme waves (rogue waves) form in an integrable system like KdV, where invariant tori and nearly conserved Fourier moduli preclude energy-exchange-based mechanisms, and to identify the dominant mechanism in the weakly nonlinear regime.
AI.method: Combines Birkhoff normal form analysis (to exploit stability of integrable dynamics) with probabilistic arguments (large deviations theory) to control the probability of phase quasi-synchronization over long timescales.
AI.result: Establishes a large deviations principle for the supremum of the solution over polynomial timescales t ≤ ε^{-n}, identifying leading-order asymptotics of the probability of observing unusually large amplitudes. Shows dispersive focusing (phase quasi-synchronization) dominates over coherent structures in the weakly nonlinear regime.
AI.conclusion: In the integrable KdV setting, extreme waves form primarily through dispersive focusing (quasi-synchronization of many phases) rather than resonant energy exchange, and this mechanism can be rigorously analyzed using Birkhoff normal forms combined with probabilistic methods over long timescales.

title: A Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager principle: Instantaneous residual minimization with gauge momentum for nonlinear parametrizations of PDE solutions
summary:
Dirac-Frenkel instantaneous residual minimization evolves nonlinear parametrizations of PDE solutions in time, but ill-conditioning can render the parameter dynamics non-unique.
  We interpret this non-uniqueness as a gauge freedom: nullspace directions that leave the
  time derivative unchanged can be used to select better-conditioned parameter velocities.
  Building on Onsager's minimum-dissipation principle, we introduce a history variable -- interpretable as momentum -- and inject it only along the nullspace directions.
  The resulting Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager dynamics preserve instantaneous residual minimization, in contrast to standard regularization that can introduce bias, while promoting temporally smooth parameter evolutions.
  Examples demonstrate that the approach leads to increased robustness in singular and near-singular regimes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Dirac-Frenkel-Onsager (DFO) method, which uses a momentum-like history variable injected along nullspace directions to resolve ill-conditioning in nonlinear parametrizations of PDE solutions, preserving residual minimization while promoting temporal smoothness and robustness.
AI.motivation: Ill-conditioning in nonlinear parameter dynamics (e.g., Dirac-Frenkel variational principle) leads to non-unique parameter velocities, causing numerical instability and lack of robustness.
AI.method: The authors interpret non-uniqueness as gauge freedom and, inspired by Onsager's minimum-dissipation principle, introduce a momentum-like history variable injected only along nullspace directions to regularize without biasing the instantaneous residual minimization.
AI.result: The DFO dynamics preserve instantaneous residual minimization (unlike standard regularization) and promote temporally smooth parameter evolutions, leading to increased robustness in singular and near-singular regimes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed gauge-corrected approach effectively handles ill-conditioning in nonlinear parametrizations without introducing bias, offering a principled alternative to standard regularization techniques.

title: Combined spatially and temporally multiplexed photonic reservoir computer with a diffractively coupled VCSEL-array
summary: We report and analyse the classification performance of an experimental hybrid spatio-temporal photonic reservoir computer based upon a free-space VCSEL array. We demonstrate experimentally the enhancement of spatial-only reservoir operation, featuring the diffractive coupling of lasers in an external cavity, by exploiting up to 88 virtual nodes with time multiplexing. We analyse the dependance of performance on the spatial and virtual node number, and achieve an improvement for both spatial- and temporal-only reservoirs with a reduced test error of 0.026 in a classification task. Further, given the high bandwidth of the non-linear laser transformation, we demonstrate the expansion of a 12 spatial node network to a 968 node network, operating at an input time of 17.6ns, maintaining high processing speed and improving network scalability and performance.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates an experimental hybrid spatio-temporal photonic reservoir computer using a VCSEL array with diffractive coupling, achieving enhanced classification performance by combining 12 spatial nodes with 88 virtual time-multiplexed nodes, reducing test error to 0.026 and scaling to 968 total nodes operating at 17.6ns input time.
AI.motivation: To improve the performance and scalability of photonic reservoir computing by combining spatial and temporal processing in a single hybrid system, addressing limitations of purely spatial or temporal reservoir computers.
AI.method: Experimental implementation of a hybrid spatio-temporal photonic reservoir computer using a free-space VCSEL array with diffractive coupling in an external cavity, enhanced by time multiplexing with up to 88 virtual nodes.
AI.result: Achieved reduced test error of 0.026 on a classification task; expanded from 12 spatial nodes to 968 total nodes (spatial + virtual) operating at 17.6ns input time while maintaining high processing speed.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid spatio-temporal photonic reservoir computing improves both performance and scalability over purely spatial or temporal approaches, leveraging the high bandwidth of nonlinear laser transformations.

title: Asymptotic profiles for the Cauchy problem of semilinear beam equation with two variable coefficients in the subcritical case
summary: In this paper, we consider the Cauchy problem for the semilinear beam equation in the subcritical case. We prove an asymptotic stability result of self-similar solutions of the associated parabolic problem. The proof of our results are based in the use of fine energy estimates in weighted spaces rewritten in the parabolic self-similar variables.

AI.tldr: The paper studies semilinear beam equations and proves asymptotic stability of self-similar solutions from the associated parabolic problem using energy estimates in weighted spaces.
AI.motivation: To establish asymptotic stability results for self-similar solutions of the semilinear beam equation in the subcritical case, which is an important aspect of understanding long-time behavior of solutions.
AI.method: The proof relies on fine energy estimates in weighted spaces, reformulated using parabolic self-similar variables.
AI.result: An asymptotic stability result of self-similar solutions of the associated parabolic problem is proven.
AI.conclusion: The semilinear beam equation in the subcritical case admits asymptotically stable self-similar solutions linked to the parabolic counterpart.

title: Beyond Independence: on Jointly Normal Priors in Bayesian Inversion
summary: We consider joint inversion for two or more unknown parameters from observational data in the Bayesian framework. Standard approaches often either treat the parameters as independent or impose structural similarity through regularisation terms that can be difficult to interpret statistically. We instead construct jointly Gaussian prior models with prescribed Gaussian marginals, so that correlation between the parameters can be incorporated without altering the marginal prior distributions. We propose a joint covariance construction that preserves the marginals, allows spatially varying cross-correlation, and supports uncertainty and inference in the correlation itself. The construction is valid for any strict contraction encoding the desired cross-correlation and is optimal in a canonical correlation sense under the principal square root factorisation. We demonstrate the method using prior sampling and several inference examples: a low-dimensional illustrative example and two higher-dimensional examples, including a PDE-constrained problem. The examples highlight both the potential pitfalls of ignoring or neglecting uncertainty in the correlation as well as reinforcing a key principle of the Bayesian paradigm: unknown quantities included in a model should be treated as random variables.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method for joint Bayesian inversion that constructs jointly Gaussian priors with prescribed marginal distributions and adjustable cross-correlation, preserving marginals while allowing spatially varying correlation and uncertainty quantification in the correlation itself.
AI.motivation: Standard joint inversion approaches either treat parameters as independent or impose structural similarity via regularization terms that are statistically hard to interpret. The authors aim to incorporate correlation between parameters without altering their marginal prior distributions, addressing the need for a statistically principled and interpretable framework.
AI.method: The authors construct jointly Gaussian prior models with prescribed Gaussian marginals. They propose a joint covariance construction that preserves marginals, allows spatially varying cross-correlation, and supports inference on the correlation itself. The construction is valid for any strict contraction encoding cross-correlation and is optimal in a canonical correlation sense under the principal square root factorization.
AI.result: Demonstrated via prior sampling and inference examples: a low-dimensional illustrative case and two higher-dimensional examples (including a PDE-constrained problem). Results highlight pitfalls of ignoring correlation uncertainty and reinforce the Bayesian principle that unknown quantities should be treated as random variables.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method enables statistically interpretable joint inversion with flexible correlation structures, and the examples underscore the importance of quantifying uncertainty in cross-correlation parameters.

title: Robin nullity and asymptotic geometry of the critical hyperbolic catenoid
comment: 18 pages; Keywords: Free boundary minimal surfaces; Hyperbolic catenoid; Jacobi operator; Robin nullity; Morse index; Spectral geometry; Parameter-criticality
summary:
For each parameter $a>1$, the critical hyperbolic catenoid $Σ_a$ is a rotationally symmetric, free boundary minimal annulus in a geodesic ball $B^3(r(a))\subset\mathbb{H}^3$. The Morse index of $Σ_a$ is at least $4$ by Medvedev [7], who conjectures equality. In this paper we identify a new geometric and spectral phenomenon for the family $\{Σ_a\}_{a>1}$, which we call "parameter-criticality", and study its consequences for the Robin spectrum. Specifically, we prove two main results:
  (I) Parameter-criticality (Theorem 1.5). The boundary radius $r(a)$ is non-monotone on $(1,\infty)$: it satisfies $r'(1^+)<0$ and $r(a)=\frac{3}{2}\log a+d_\infty+o(1)$ as $a\to\infty$ with $d_\infty=\log[Γ(1/4)/Γ(3/4)]-\frac{1}{2}\log(2π)$ (Theorem 1.4). Hence there exists a parameter-critical value $a^\sharp\in(1,\infty)$ with $r'(a^\sharp)=0$.
  (II) Robin nullity jump (Theorem 1.6). At every such $a^\sharp$, the Robin nullity of $Σ_{a^\sharp}$ satisfies $\text{nul}(L_{Σ_{a^\sharp}})\geq 3$, with an additional kernel element in mode $k=0$ generated by the parametric variation field $j_a=\langle\partial_aΦ_a,ν\rangle_L|_{a=a^\sharp}$, which we show is non-vanishing at the catenoid neck via the closed-form $j_a(0)=1/(2\sqrt{a^2-1})$.
  The argument requires the limit $r_0:=\lim_{a\to 1^+}r(a)$ characterized as the unique positive solution of the transcendental equation $\tanh(r_0)\,\tanh(2r_0/\sqrt{3})=\sqrt{3}/2$ (Theorem 1.3), giving a clean parametrization of the degeneration $Σ_a\toΣ_1$. The Robin nullity of $Σ_a$ in mode $|k|=1$ is shown to equal $2$ (Proposition 1.1); this extends to the hyperbolic setting the mode-by-mode Fourier decomposition technique of Devyver [2] for the Euclidean critical catenoid, and is used in the proof of (II) to identify the extra kernel as a mode-$k=0$ phenomenon.

AI.tldr: The paper studies a family of minimal annuli (hyperbolic catenoids) in geodesic balls of hyperbolic space, discovering a "parameter-critical" point where the boundary radius is stationary, leading to a jump in Robin nullity (from 2 to ≥3) due to a mode-0 kernel element generated by parametric variation.
AI.motivation: Medvedev conjectured that the Morse index of the critical hyperbolic catenoid Σ_a is exactly 4 (at least 4 known). The authors aim to understand the geometric/spectral structure more deeply and test this conjecture by studying parametric dependence.
AI.method: Asymptotic analysis of boundary radius r(a) as a→1⁺ and a→∞; transcendental equation for limit r₀; mode-by-mode Fourier decomposition (extending Devyver's technique to hyperbolic setting); explicit computation of the parametric variation field j_a.
AI.result: (I) r(a) is non-monotone, so ∃ a critical a^♯ where r'(a^♯)=0. (II) At such a^♯, Robin nullity ≥3, with an extra kernel element in mode k=0 from the variation field j_a, which is non-vanishing at the neck (j_a(0)=1/(2√(a²-1))).
AI.conclusion: Parameter-criticality induces a nullity jump, revealing a new spectral phenomenon for the Robin problem on hyperbolic catenoids; the extra mode-0 kernel supports refining the Morse index conjecture.

title: Linking PageRank, Time Reversal, and Policy Evaluation
summary: We establish a connection between policy evaluation in Markov decision processes and PageRank in network analysis. For a fixed policy, we show that the value function of a discounted Markov decision process can be obtained, up to an explicit rescaling, from the PageRank vector of a suitably defined time-reversed Markov chain. In this correspondence, the discount factor plays the role of the teleportation parameter, while rewards induce the restart distribution. Beyond the irreducible case, invoking quasi-stationary distributions and Doob $h$-transforms, we prove a general decomposition theorem showing that policy evaluation for arbitrary finite MDPs reduces to a collection of PageRank problems on the recurrent and transient components of the policy-induced Markov chain. This framework naturally extends to undiscounted MDPs with terminal states and to transition-dependent rewards. We conclude by showing efficiency of our approach on a numerical example of a sticky random walk on large deterministic and random graphs.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes a formal connection between policy evaluation in MDPs and PageRank, showing that value functions can be obtained from PageRank vectors via time-reversed Markov chains, with extensions to general finite MDPs via quasi-stationary distributions and Doob h-transforms.
AI.motivation: To bridge reinforcement learning (policy evaluation) and network analysis (PageRank), potentially enabling cross-fertilization of algorithms and insights between the two fields.
AI.method: The authors prove that for a fixed policy, the value function of a discounted MDP equals (up to rescaling) the PageRank vector of a time-reversed Markov chain, where discount factor becomes teleportation parameter and rewards define restart distribution. For general finite MDPs, they use quasi-stationary distributions and Doob h-transforms to decompose policy evaluation into PageRank problems on recurrent and transient components.
AI.result: The framework extends to undiscounted MDPs with terminal states and transition-dependent rewards. Numerical experiments on a sticky random walk on large deterministic and random graphs demonstrate computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Policy evaluation in MDPs reduces to solving PageRank problems, enabling the use of efficient PageRank algorithms for reinforcement learning tasks.

title: Nonlocal Approximation Principle for Entropy Solutions of Scalar Conservation Laws
summary: We establish a general nonlocal approximation principle for the entropy solutions of scalar conservation laws on $\mathbb{R}$. More precisely, we show that the entropy solution to a nonnegative initial datum can be obtained as a weak-star limit of a corresponding scalar nonlocal conservation law. The flux function of the nonlocal conservation law depends on suitable spatial averages of the density. The proof is based on a reformulation on the Hamilton--Jacobi level: working with the primitives, we identify the limit via the stability properties of viscosity solutions; we then recover the entropy solution using the classical relation between Hamilton--Jacobi equations and scalar conservation laws. We further show that the approximation extends, after a suitable shift, to sign-changing initial data, and we prove a quantitative convergence estimate for convex fluxes in terms of the first moments of the nonlocal kernels. This result makes it possible to define entropy solutions for general fluxes using their nonlocal approximations, which satisfy the requirement for a finite speed of mass propagation, a key feature of hyperbolic conservation laws.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that entropy solutions of scalar conservation laws can be obtained as weak-star limits of nonlocal conservation laws, using a Hamilton-Jacobi reformulation approach. The approximation works for nonnegative and sign-changing initial data, with quantitative convergence estimates for convex fluxes.
AI.motivation: To establish a rigorous nonlocal approximation principle for entropy solutions of conservation laws, enabling the definition of entropy solutions via nonlocal approximations that inherently satisfy finite speed of mass propagation.
AI.method: The proof reformulates the problem at the Hamilton-Jacobi level (working with primitives), identifies the limit via stability properties of viscosity solutions, then recovers entropy solutions using the classical HJ-to-conservation law relation. A quantitative convergence estimate is provided for convex fluxes using first moments of nonlocal kernels.
AI.result: Entropy solutions can be obtained as weak-star limits of nonlocal conservation laws. The approximation extends to sign-changing initial data with a suitable shift. Quantitative convergence rates are established for convex fluxes.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a way to define entropy solutions for general fluxes using their nonlocal approximations, which naturally satisfy the finite speed of mass propagation—a key hyperbolic feature.

title: Local Geometry of Least Squares for Unmixing Signals with Parameter-Dependent Dictionaries
comment: 13 pages, 11 figures. Submitted to IEEE TSP
summary: Modeling signals as linear combinations of atoms from a dictionary is ubiquitous in modern signal processing. In the finite-dimensional setting, whenever atoms depend nonlinearly upon unknown parameters, the signal model is said to be separable. In this work, we study least-squares reconstruction of separable signals and establish a unified theoretical framework for their analysis. We introduce the unmixing metric, a distance that captures the distinct roles and sensitivities of linear and nonlinear parameters, and establish local convergence and stability guarantees under its topology. We then analyze variable projection from a geometric perspective, showing that it corresponds to restricting the optimization to the manifold of optimal linear parameters. This viewpoint provides a principled explanation for the improved algorithmic behavior of variable projection observed in practice, and produces sharp theoretical guarantees. The generic theory for separable problems is specialized to the case of point spread function (PSF) unmixing. We introduce a parametric notion of coherence and show that support separation directly controls both the size of the convergence region and the stability of recovery. Numerical experiments corroborate the theoretical predictions and demonstrate the practical relevance of the proposed framework.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a unified theoretical framework for least-squares reconstruction of separable signals (where atoms depend nonlinearly on unknown parameters), introducing the unmixing metric for theoretical analysis and providing sharp guarantees for variable projection, with applications to PSF unmixing.
AI.motivation: To establish a unified theoretical framework for analyzing separable signal models where atoms depend nonlinearly on unknown parameters, and to provide principled explanations and guarantees for the variable projection method commonly used in practice.
AI.method: The authors introduce the "unmixing metric" to capture the distinct roles of linear and nonlinear parameters, analyze variable projection from a geometric perspective (as optimization on the manifold of optimal linear parameters), and specialize the theory to PSF unmixing using parametric coherence.
AI.result: Local convergence and stability guarantees under the unmixing metric topology; sharp theoretical guarantees for variable projection; for PSF unmixing, support separation directly controls convergence region size and recovery stability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework provides principled explanations for variable projection's practical success and yields sharp theoretical guarantees, with numerical experiments confirming the predictions.

title: Quantitative symmetry-breaking and nonlinear harmonic generation in plasmonics
summary: We develop a quantitative mathematical theory that offers new perspectives on nonlinear harmonic generation in plasmonic structures arising from symmetry breaking. Focusing on second harmonic generation--the most fundamental process and the most extensively studied owing to its practical significance--we establish a theoretical framework that can be readily extended to higher-order harmonics. We investigate the plasmonic system in the static regime using a columnar nanowire with \(n\)-fold rotational symmetry (\(n \in \mathbb{N}\)) and construct a phenomenological model in which the second harmonic response originates from nonlinear sources confined to a selvedge region near the surface. By introducing a notion of symmetry degree grounded in group theory, we precisely quantify the second harmonic generation in terms of multipolar contributions. Our theory complements existing physical descriptions of this practically important phenomenon and provides a rigorous account of how nonlinear optical efficiency depends on shape, size, symmetry, and defects in plasmonic structures.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a quantitative mathematical theory for nonlinear harmonic generation in plasmonic nanostructures, focusing on second harmonic generation from symmetry breaking. Using a columnar nanowire model with n-fold rotational symmetry and a surface-confined nonlinear source, the authors introduce a group-theoretic "symmetry degree" to quantify multipolar contributions to second harmonic generation.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding how symmetry breaking influences nonlinear harmonic generation in plasmonic structures, particularly second harmonic generation, and to precisely quantify how shape, size, symmetry, and defects affect nonlinear optical efficiency.
AI.method: The authors develop a phenomenological model using a columnar nanowire with n-fold rotational symmetry, treating nonlinear sources as confined to a selvedge region near the surface. They introduce a "symmetry degree" concept based on group theory to quantify multipolar contributions to second harmonic generation.
AI.result: A precise, group-theoretic quantification of second harmonic generation in terms of multipolar contributions, establishing how nonlinear optical efficiency depends on structural parameters like shape, size, symmetry, and defects.
AI.conclusion: The theory complements existing physical descriptions of nonlinear harmonic generation and provides a rigorous account of the relationship between structural properties and nonlinear optical efficiency in plasmonic systems.

title: Modular Forms and Numerical Explorations of Rational Approximations to $ζ(3)$
summary: We revisit Beukers' modular-form proof of the irrationality of $ζ(3)$ from the point of view of the auxiliary weight two modular form. For the Fricke group $Γ_0(6)^\star$, we show that Beukers' choice is not isolated: it belongs to a one-parameter affine family. These approximations have the same exponential decay as the classical Apéry approximations and satisfy the same denominator-growth estimate needed in Beukers' irrationality argument. We then apply the same construction to several other genus-zero Fricke groups.

AI.tldr: The paper revisits Beukers' proof of the irrationality of ζ(3) via modular forms, showing Beukers' choice is part of a one-parameter family for the Fricke group Γ₀(6)*, and extends this construction to other genus-zero Fricke groups.
AI.motivation: To revisit Beukers' modular-form proof of the irrationality of ζ(3) and show that the auxiliary weight 2 modular form used is not unique but belongs to a broader family, and to explore whether similar constructions work for other Fricke groups.
AI.method: The authors study the Fricke group Γ₀(6)* and show Beukers' choice corresponds to a one-parameter affine family of weight 2 modular forms. They apply the same construction to other genus-zero Fricke groups.
AI.result: Beukers' choice is not isolated—it belongs to a one-parameter family. These approximations have the same exponential decay as Apéry's approximations and satisfy the same denominator-growth estimates. The construction also applies to other genus-zero Fricke groups.
AI.conclusion: The modular-form approach to the irrationality of ζ(3) admits natural generalizations via families of modular forms on Fricke groups, potentially extending to other irrationality results.

title: Unstable free boundary problems in optimal control theory: existence and regularity
summary: We establish the first general regularity result for constrained optimal control problems arising naturally in mathematical physics and mathematical biology. Namely, we prove that for a large class of problems of the form ``maximise $\int ψ(Θ_m)-c\int m$ where $-ΔΘ_m=mΘ_m+B(x,Θ_m)$, under the constraint $0\leq m\leq 1$ a.e.", the solution $m^*$ is bang-bang, in the sense that $m^*=χ_{E^*}$, and that $\partial E^*$ is smooth up to a $(d-2)$-dimensional subset. Moreover, we prove that the solutions to the volume constrained problem ``maximise $\int ψ(Θ_m)$ where $-ΔΘ_m=mΘ_m+B(x,Θ_m)$, under the constraint $0\leq m\leq 1$ a.e and $\int m=m_0$" are bang-bang in the sense that $m^*=χ_{E^*}$ and that, in the two-dimensional case, $\partial E^*$ is a finite union of smooth curves. This is done via reduction to an unstable free boundary problem, the regularity analysis of which was pioneered by Monneau \& Weiss and Chanillo, Kenig \& To. In our case, the free boundary is not minimising, and the laplacian of the state function is sign-changing, which creates significant difficulties, in particular regarding the non-degeneracy of blow-ups. This requires a new approach blending tools from optimal control theory, free boundary and measure theory to establish the regularity of the free boundary.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that optimal controls for a class of constrained PDE-constrained problems are bang-bang (take only values 0 or 1), and the resulting free boundaries are smooth except for low-dimensional singular sets.
AI.motivation: To establish the first general regularity result for constrained optimal control problems arising in mathematical physics and biology, where solutions are expected to be bang-bang (i.e., 0-1 valued) but rigorous regularity of the resulting free boundaries was lacking.
AI.method: The authors reduce the problem to an unstable free boundary problem, leveraging tools from optimal control theory, free boundary analysis, and measure theory. They address difficulties including sign-changing Laplacians and non-minimizing free boundaries via a novel blend of techniques.
AI.result: For unconstrained volume: solution m* is bang-bang (m* = χ_{E*}) and ∂E* is smooth except on a (d-2)-dimensional subset. For the volume-constrained problem (∫m = m_0): m* is also bang-bang, and in 2D, ∂E* is a finite union of smooth curves.
AI.conclusion: The results provide foundational regularity theory for constrained optimal control problems in PDE-constrained settings, demonstrating that bang-bang structure and smooth free boundaries hold under broad conditions despite significant technical challenges.

title: HyCOP: Hybrid Composition Operators for Interpretable Learning of PDEs
summary: We introduce HyCOP, a modular framework that learns parametric PDE solution operators by composing simple modules (advection, diffusion, learned closures, boundary handling) in a query-conditioned way. Rather than learning a monolithic map, HyCOP learns a policy over short programs - which module to apply and for how long - conditioned on regime features and state statistics. Modules may be numerical sub-solvers or learned components, enabling hybrid surrogates evaluated at arbitrary query times without autoregressive rollout. Across diverse PDE benchmarks, HyCOP produces interpretable programs, delivers order-of-magnitude OOD improvements over monolithic neural operators, and supports modular transfer through dictionary updates (e.g., boundary swaps, residual enrichment). Our theory characterizes expressivity and gives an error decomposition that separates composition error from module error and doubles as a process-level diagnostic.

AI.tldr: HyCOP is a modular framework that learns PDE solution operators by composing simple modules (advection, diffusion, learned closures, boundary handling) conditioned on query features, enabling interpretable programs with order-of-magnitude OOD improvements over monolithic neural operators.
AI.motivation: Existing neural operators learn monolithic maps that lack interpretability, struggle with out-of-distribution generalization, and require autoregressive rollout for arbitrary query times. The authors aim to address these limitations through a compositional approach.
AI.method: HyCOP learns a policy over short programs—selecting which module (numerical or learned) to apply and for how long—conditioned on regime features and state statistics. Modules include advection, diffusion, learned closures, and boundary handling components.
AI.result: HyCOP produces interpretable programs, achieves order-of-magnitude improvements in out-of-distribution generalization over monolithic neural operators, and supports modular transfer through dictionary updates (boundary swaps, residual enrichment).
AI.conclusion: The compositional approach yields better generalization and interpretability, with a theoretical error decomposition separating composition error from module error that doubles as a process-level diagnostic tool.

title: Gagliardo-Nirenberg type inequalities with a BMO term and fractional Laplacians
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2512.11576
summary: An improvement of a {\em Global (strong) Gagliardo-Nienberg inequality with a BMO term} is established by replacing local derivatives by {\em and fractional Laplacians.} Local versions are also given.

AI.tldr: This paper improves a Global Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality with a BMO term by replacing local derivatives with fractional Laplacians, and also provides local versions.
AI.motivation: To improve the existing Global Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality by incorporating fractional Laplacians instead of local derivatives, which may yield sharper or more general results.
AI.method: The authors replace local derivatives with fractional Laplacians in the existing Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality framework, and derive both global and local versions of the inequality.
AI.result: An improved Global Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality with a BMO term using fractional Laplacians, along with corresponding local versions.
AI.conclusion: The use of fractional Laplacians enhances the classical Gagliardo-Nirenberg inequality, and local versions are also established.

title: Compactness of weighted Sobolev trace operators and non-linear Steklov problems
summary: We prove the compactness of weighted Sobolev trace operators in outward cuspidal domains by using composition operators on Sobolev spaces. This result allows us to formulate the non-linear Steklov problem in outward cuspidal domains in a correct functional setting and to establish the existence of its non-trivial solution.

AI.tldr: The paper proves compactness of weighted Sobolev trace operators in outward cuspidal domains, enabling correct functional formulation and existence of non-trivial solutions for the non-linear Steklov problem.
AI.motivation: To establish a proper functional analysis framework for solving non-linear Steklov problems in outward cuspidal domains, which have challenging boundary geometries.
AI.method: Uses composition operators on Sobolev spaces to prove compactness of weighted Sobolev trace operators in outward cuspidal domains.
AI.result: Compactness of weighted Sobolev trace operators is proved, leading to correct functional setting and existence of non-trivial solutions for the non-linear Steklov problem.
AI.conclusion: The compactness result enables rigorous analysis of non-linear Steklov problems in outward cuspidal domains, with proven existence of non-trivial solutions.

title: Existence of dipoles of Klein-Gordon-Zakharov system
summary:
In this paper, we study the long time behavior of solutions of Klein-Gordon-Zakharov system. We show that there exists a solution with special characteristics, which we shall refer to as a dipole solution, that is, there exists a solution $\vec{u}$ such that
  $$\left\|\vec{u}(t)-\sum_{k=1}^{2}\vec{R}_{k}\right\|_{X}
  \to 0 \, \, \text{as}\, \, t\to \infty,$$
  where $\vec{R}_{k}$ represents a solitary wave for each $k$, with a translation $z_k$ with respect to its position, satisfying that $$|z_1(t)-z_2(t)| \sim 2\log(t)\, \, \text{as} \, \, t\to \infty.$$
