  Our approach will initially focus on the spectral analysis of the Hamiltonian operator associated with our system. Subsequently, we aim to establish a coercivity estimate that will allow us to derive conditions ensuring the existence of our solution. It is important to note that, in this problem, our objective is to obtain approximate solutions by solving a final data problem. These approximate solutions will then be used, through uniform estimates and compactness results, to derive the desired conclusions via density arguments.

AI.tldr: This paper proves the existence of a dipole solution for the Klein-Gordon-Zakharov system, consisting of two solitary waves whose distance grows like 2log(t) as time goes to infinity.
AI.motivation: To understand the long-time behavior of solutions to the Klein-Gordon-Zakharov system, specifically the existence of multi-soliton structures (dipole solutions) that exhibit particular asymptotic dynamics.
AI.method: The approach involves: (1) spectral analysis of the associated Hamiltonian operator, (2) establishing a coercivity estimate for existence conditions, (3) constructing approximate solutions by solving a final data problem, and (4) using uniform estimates and compactness arguments with density arguments to obtain the final solution.
AI.result: The authors prove the existence of a dipole solution where two solitary waves separate asymptotically at a rate of 2log(t) as t→∞, with the solution converging to the sum of the two solitary waves in the X-norm.
AI.conclusion: The Klein-Gordon-Zakharov system admits dipole solutions with logarithmically diverging solitary waves, demonstrating complex multi-soliton dynamics in this system.

title: Smooth and stable Euler implosions
summary: We construct a new class of self-similar implosion profiles for the multi-dimensional compressible Euler equations. These profiles are smooth, genuinely non-isentropic, radially/spherically symmetric, and have explicit (closed-form) similarity exponents. We prove that the exact Euler solution corresponding to the ground state implosion profile is stable to radially symmetric perturbations, as a solution to the full nonlinear compressible Euler equations, modulo a one-dimensional compatibility condition on the initial data. For perturbations of the Euler solution corresponding to the ground state implosion profile of a monatomic or diatomic gas, that do not obey any symmetry assumptions, we provide a complete characterization of the set of initial data that yield nonlinear stability.

AI.tldr: The paper constructs new smooth, self-similar implosion profiles for compressible Euler equations, proves their stability under radially symmetric perturbations (with a compatibility condition), and fully characterizes initial data yielding nonlinear stability for monatomic/diatomic gases without symmetry assumptions.
AI.motivation: To construct genuinely non-isentropic, explicit self-similar implosion solutions for the multi-dimensional compressible Euler equations and analyze their stability properties.
AI.method: Construction of new self-similar implosion profiles with closed-form similarity exponents; stability analysis via nonlinear Euler equations with radial symmetry constraints and a one-dimensional compatibility condition.
AI.result: Smooth, non-isentropic, radially/spherically symmetric implosion profiles with explicit similarity exponents are obtained. Ground state solutions are stable to radial perturbations under a compatibility condition. For monatomic/diatomic gases, a complete characterization of stable initial data without symmetry assumptions is provided.
AI.conclusion: The new self-similar profiles expand the known solution space for Euler implosions, and the stability results offer rigorous insights into the conditions under which such implosions persist under general perturbations.

title: Classical limit of the Pauli-Fierz dynamics
summary: We study the Schrödinger evolution generated by the Pauli-Fierz Hamiltonian, a model for nonrelativistic quantum electrodynamics, in the classical limit $\hbar \rightarrow 0$. In this regime, we rigorously derive the Newton-Maxwell equations of classical electrodynamics as effective dynamics approximating the time evolution. Our result complements prior work by an alternative derivation that provides explicit estimates on the rate of convergence, justifying the validity of the approximation for a special class of initial data.

AI.tldr: This paper rigorously derives the Newton-Maxwell equations from the Pauli-Fierz Hamiltonian in the classical limit ℏ → 0, providing explicit convergence estimates for a special class of initial data.
AI.motivation: To rigorously derive the Newton-Maxwell equations of classical electrodynamics as effective dynamics approximating the Schrödinger evolution generated by the Pauli-Fierz Hamiltonian in the classical limit, and to provide explicit estimates on the rate of convergence.
AI.method: The authors study the Schrödinger evolution generated by the Pauli-Fierz Hamiltonian in the classical limit ℏ → 0, using an alternative derivation that provides explicit estimates on the rate of convergence for a special class of initial data.
AI.result: The Newton-Maxwell equations of classical electrodynamics are rigorously derived as effective dynamics approximating the time evolution, with explicit convergence rate estimates complementing prior work.
AI.conclusion: The derivation provides a rigorous justification for the validity of the classical approximation in the ℏ → 0 regime, supported by quantitative convergence estimates for specific initial conditions.
